params.text:
Severe allergic reaction: anaphylaxis

Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that can develop within minutes of contact with a trigger, such as certain foods, insect stings or medicines. Signs include a widespread rash, swelling of the face, lips, tongue or throat, difficulty or noisy breathing, a sense of something being seriously wrong, and collapse.

Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency. Call 999 immediately and tell the call handler that you suspect anaphylaxis, so that the right help is sent quickly.

Adrenaline auto-injectors and asthma attacks

Many people at risk of anaphylaxis carry an adrenaline auto-injector, such as an EpiPen. If the person has one, help them use it without delay, following the instructions on the device, and note the time. A second dose may be given after five minutes if there is no improvement and another injector is available.

  • Position them: help them sit up if breathing is hard, or lie flat with legs raised if they feel faint.
  • Asthma attack: help the person sit up and take their reliever inhaler, usually blue, one puff at a time.
  • Call 999: if the inhaler does not help, symptoms get worse, or they cannot speak in full sentences.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on severe allergic reactions and asthma attacks.
params.textField: A severe, life-threatening allergic reaction is called *anaphylaxis*. If you suspect it, call *999* straight away. If the person carries an adrenaline *auto-injector*, help them use it without delay. Someone having an asthma attack should sit up and use their reliever inhaler, which is usually *blue*. A second dose of adrenaline may be given after *five* minutes if there is no improvement.
